It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 93 actions; a strong score needs about 29 or fewer. You are 3.2x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 76 actions returned the board to a state already seen 76 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 76 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: I see the board changed. The white block is now overlapping with the character. I can see some gray blocks (G) appearing, which suggests progress. Let me check the current state to see if the level completed or if more actions are needed.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("Current nodes:")
for n in seg:
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  color={n['color']}, rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

red_count = sum(1 for n in seg if n['color'] == 'R')
g_count = sum(1 for n in seg if n['color'] == 'G')
n_count = sum(1 for n in seg if n['color'] == 'N')
print(f"\nRed: {red_count}, DarkGray(G): {g_count}, N pieces: {n_count}")

# Check top bar
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'W' and min([p[0] for p in n['boundary']]) == 0:
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"\nTop bar W: cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Top bar c: cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

print(f"\nLevel completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Good progress! The red blocks are now gone (0 red), and there are 2 gray (G) blocks. The top bar shows W at cols 16-22 (7 pixels) and c at cols 23-47 (25 pixels). This is similar to the step 16 configuration!
But the level is not completed yet. Let me compare with the step 16 configuration:
- W at rows 32-46, cols 41-55 (should be 38-52 at step 16)
- N pieces: 4 pieces
- G blocks: 2 at rows 35-37 cols 38-40 and rows 41-43 cols 38-40
Wait, W is at cols 41-55, not 38-52. That's 3 columns to the right. Let me check if I moved too far right.
Actually, looking at the step 16 config, W was at cols 38-52. But now W is at cols 41-55. So I moved 3 columns too far right.
Also, the top bar shows W at cols 16-22 (7 pixels), which is the same as step 16. So the progress bar is in the same state.
But there are still N pieces and G blocks. The goal might be to convert all N pieces to something else, or to position W correctly and press SPACE again.
Let me try pressing SPACE again to see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try SPACE again
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE to continue progress')
action(['SPACE'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
